questions.question:
Some tables are shelves. Some shelves are chairs. All chairs are benches. Which of the following conclusion can be deduced from the preceding sentences?
i. At least one bench is a table
ii. At least one shelf is a bench
iii. At least one chair is a table
iv. All benches are chairs

A. Only i
B. Only ii
C. Only ii and iii
D. Only iv
questions.to_find: Which conclusion(s) logically follow from the three given categorical statements

understand.plain: We have three statements forming a chain: some tables overlap with shelves, some shelves overlap with chairs, and all chairs are a subset of benches. We must test each conclusion against what the premises actually guarantee. Only statement ii is a guaranteed deduction: since some shelves are chairs and all chairs are benches, at least one shelf must be a bench.

keywords.explain: A form of logical reasoning where a conclusion is drawn from two or more premises using categorical relationships.
keywords.example: All cats are mammals; some animals are cats; therefore some animals are mammals.

keywords.explain: A particular affirmative: at least one member of set A belongs to set B, but not necessarily all.
keywords.example: Some shelves are chairs means at least one shelf is also a chair.

keywords.explain: A universal affirmative: every member of set A is also a member of set B, making A a subset of B.
keywords.example: All chairs are benches means the chair set is entirely inside the bench set.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 420 200' width='420' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='210' cy='100' rx='170' ry='75' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.5'/><text x='350' y='105' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Benches</text><ellipse cx='185' cy='105' rx='110' ry='55' fill='none' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.5'/><text x='120' y='92' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Chairs (subset)</text><ellipse cx='150' cy='100' rx='60' ry='35' fill='none' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.6'/><text x='150' y='97' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12'>Some</text><text x='150' y='112' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12'>Shelves</text><ellipse cx='90' cy='88' rx='40' ry='25' fill='none' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><text x='90' y='86' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle' font-size='11'>Some</text><text x='90' y='99' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle' font-size='11'>Tables</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Nested ellipses showing Benches (largest) containing all Chairs, with some Shelves overlapping Chairs and some Tables overlapping Shelves.

given.aim: Identify which of the four conclusions necessarily follows from the three premises

terms.meaning: The set of tables and the set of shelves have at least one common element.
terms.example: There exists at least one object that is both a table and a shelf.
terms.connects: Establishes a partial overlap between tables and shelves; does NOT mean all tables are shelves.

terms.meaning: At least one shelf is also a chair.
terms.example: There exists at least one object that is both a shelf and a chair.
terms.connects: Combined with Premise 3, this guarantees at least one shelf is also a bench.

terms.meaning: The entire chair set is a subset of the bench set; no chair exists outside the bench set.
terms.example: Every chair object is also a bench object.
terms.connects: This universal statement allows us to extend properties of chairs to benches.

given.plan: Test each conclusion using Venn diagram reasoning. For a conclusion to be valid it must hold true in ALL possible arrangements consistent with the premises, not just some.

steps.title: Test conclusion ii: At least one shelf is a bench
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (B -> C), conclude (A -> C)
steps.apply: Premise 2 says some shelves ARE chairs. Premise 3 says ALL chairs ARE benches. So those shelves that are chairs must also be benches (by Premise 3). Therefore at least one shelf is a bench. Conclusion ii HOLDS.
steps.note: This is a valid syllogistic inference: the particular shelf that is a chair is necessarily also a bench.

steps.title: Test conclusions i, iii, iv
steps.formula_raw: "All A are B" = forall x (A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: i: Some tables are shelves, but we cannot chain further to benches because the shelf-chair overlap may not include those shelves that are also tables. Conclusion i is NOT guaranteed. iii: Some shelves are chairs, but the tables that are shelves need not be the same shelves that are chairs, so no chair must be a table. Conclusion iii is NOT guaranteed. iv: All chairs are benches does NOT mean all benches are chairs (conversion fallacy). Conclusion iv is INVALID.
steps.note: Conversion of 'All A are B' to 'All B are A' is a well-known logical fallacy.

solution.result: Only conclusion ii is a guaranteed deduction. Answer: B. Only ii.

questions.formula_note: Hypothetical syllogism chains Premise 2 (some shelves are chairs) with Premise 3 (all chairs are benches) to confirm conclusion ii; the all-A-are-B rule prevents the conversion fallacy that would make iv valid.

questions.question:
All people in a certain island are either 'Knights' or 'Knaves' and each person knows every other person's identity. Knights never lie, and Knaves ALWAYS lie.
P says "Both P and Q are Knights".
Q says "None of us are Knaves".
Which one of the following can be logically inferred from the above?

A. Both P and Q are Knights.
B. P is a knight; Q is a Knave.
C. Both P and Q are Knaves.
D. The identities of P and Q cannot be determined.
questions.answer: D. The identities of P and Q cannot be determined.
questions.to_find: The identity (Knight or Knave) of P and Q from their statements

understand.plain: On this island every person is exclusively a Knight (always truthful) or a Knave (always lies). P claims both are Knights; Q claims neither is a Knave. Note that Q's statement 'None of us are Knaves' is logically equivalent to 'Both P and Q are Knights', which is identical in meaning to what P said. We need to check each scenario for self-consistency and see if a unique assignment is forced.

keywords.explain: A person who always tells the truth on this island; every statement a Knight makes is true.
keywords.example: If P is a Knight and P says 'Both are Knights', then both must actually be Knights.

keywords.explain: A person who always lies on this island; every statement a Knave makes is false.
keywords.example: If P is a Knave and says 'Both are Knights', then the true situation is NOT 'both are Knights'.

keywords.explain: A statement that includes the speaker themselves as part of its claim, creating a constraint that must be checked for consistency.
keywords.example: P says 'Both P and Q are Knights' — this involves P themselves, so if P is a Knave the statement is false.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; the question is purely logical.

given.aim: Determine whether P and Q are Knights or Knaves, or whether it is indeterminate

terms.term: P's statement: 'Both P and Q are Knights'
terms.meaning: P asserts that P is a Knight AND Q is a Knight simultaneously.
terms.example: If true, both are Knights; if false, at least one is a Knave.
terms.connects: If P is a Knight the statement must be true; if P is a Knave the statement must be false.

terms.term: Q's statement: 'None of us are Knaves'
terms.meaning: Q asserts that neither P nor Q is a Knave, which is logically identical to 'Both P and Q are Knights'.
terms.example: Q's statement is equivalent to P's statement in logical content.
terms.connects: Both P and Q make the same logical claim, so their truth values are linked to the same proposition.

terms.meaning: An assignment is valid only if Knights' statements are true and Knaves' statements are false under that assignment.
terms.example: If we assume P = Knight and Q = Knight, we must verify both statements become true.
terms.connects: Used to eliminate impossible assignments and find valid ones.

given.plan: Enumerate all four possible identity assignments (KK, KN, NK, NN), apply consistency checks to each, and see which assignments are consistent with the given statements.

steps.title: Case 1: Both P and Q are Knights (KK)
steps.formula_raw: Two formulas are logically equivalent when they have identical truth values in every row of the truth table
steps.apply: P's statement 'Both are Knights' = TRUE (since both are actually Knights). Knight P tells the truth. Consistent. Q's statement 'None are Knaves' = TRUE. Knight Q tells the truth. Consistent. Case KK is VALID.
steps.note: Both statements are true and both speakers are Knights — self-consistent.

steps.title: Case 2: P is Knight, Q is Knave (KN)
steps.formula_raw: Two formulas are logically equivalent when they have identical truth values in every row of the truth table
steps.apply: P's statement 'Both are Knights' = FALSE (Q is actually a Knave). But P is a Knight who must tell the truth. CONTRADICTION. Case KN is INVALID.
steps.note: A Knight cannot make a false statement, so KN is ruled out.

steps.title: Case 3: P is Knave, Q is Knight (NK)
steps.formula_raw: Two formulas are logically equivalent when they have identical truth values in every row of the truth table
steps.apply: P's statement 'Both are Knights' = FALSE (P is actually a Knave). Knave P must lie, so the statement must be false. Consistent for P. Q's statement 'None are Knaves' = FALSE (P is a Knave). But Q is a Knight who must tell the truth. CONTRADICTION. Case NK is INVALID.
steps.note: Q being a Knight while P is a Knave makes Q's identical claim false, contradicting Q's Knight status.

steps.title: Case 4: Both P and Q are Knaves (NN)
steps.formula_raw: Two formulas are logically equivalent when they have identical truth values in every row of the truth table
steps.apply: P's statement 'Both are Knights' = FALSE (both are Knaves). Knave P must lie, so the statement must be false. Consistent. Q's statement 'None are Knaves' = FALSE (both are actually Knaves). Knave Q must lie. Consistent. Case NN is VALID.
steps.note: Both Knaves lying about being Knights is self-consistent.

steps.title: Conclusion: two valid cases remain
steps.formula_raw: Statement R implies statement C when every interpretation (model) that makes R true also makes C true.
steps.apply: Both KK and NN are consistent with the given statements. Since two different assignments are valid, the identities of P and Q cannot be uniquely determined.
steps.note: The statements made by P and Q are identical in logical content, so they provide no differential information to separate the two cases.

solution.result: Both KK (both Knights) and NN (both Knaves) are consistent; the identities cannot be determined. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: Truth-table consistency checks eliminate KN and NK cases; the two remaining valid models (KK and NN) mean no unique identity assignment can be deduced.

questions.question:
Consider the following three statements:
i. Some roses are red.
ii. All red flowers fade quickly.
iii. Some roses fade quickly.
Which of the following statements can be logically inferred from the above statements?

A. If (i) is true and (ii) is false, then (iii) is false.
B. If (i) is true and (ii) is false, then (iii) is true.
C. If (i) is true and (ii) is true, then (iii) is true.
D. If (i) and (ii) are false, then (iii) is false.
questions.answer: C. If (i) is true and (ii) is true, then (iii) is true.
questions.to_find: Which conditional statement about (i), (ii), and (iii) is a logically valid inference

understand.plain: This is a classic syllogism problem. Statement (iii) is a conclusion that can be derived from (i) and (ii) together via a chain of reasoning: some roses are red (i), all red flowers fade quickly (ii), so those red roses must fade quickly, meaning some roses fade quickly (iii). We need to check which option correctly captures the logical relationship among the three statements.

keywords.explain: Chaining two conditional or categorical statements to derive a new conclusion; if A implies B and B implies C, then A implies C.
keywords.example: Some roses are red + All red things fade = some roses fade (by composition).

keywords.explain: An inference of the form 'if premises P1 and P2 are true, then conclusion C must be true'; valid only when the logical chain is sound.
keywords.example: Option C says 'If (i) and (ii) are true then (iii) is true' — this is the valid chain.

keywords.explain: Asserts at least one A is B; combined with a universal 'All B are C', we can conclude at least one A is C.
keywords.example: Some roses are red + All red flowers fade = some roses fade.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 380 180' width='380' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='380' height='180' rx='10' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='130' cy='95' rx='95' ry='65' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='60' y='75' class='sub mut' font-size='13'>Roses</text><ellipse cx='210' cy='95' rx='95' ry='65' fill='none' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='280' y='75' class='sub mut' font-size='13'>Fade Quickly</text><ellipse cx='175' cy='95' rx='35' ry='28' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.7'/><text x='175' y='91' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' font-size='11'>Red</text><text x='175' y='105' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' font-size='11'>Roses</text><text x='175' y='158' class='tag mut' text-anchor='middle'>Red roses = overlap of all three sets</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram showing Roses and Fade-Quickly sets overlapping, with Red Roses (a subset of both when premises i and ii hold) in the intersection region.

given.aim: Identify which conditional relationship among statements i, ii, iii is always logically valid

terms.term: Statement i: Some roses are red
terms.meaning: Particular affirmative; there exists at least one rose that is also red.
terms.example: At least one object belongs to both the rose set and the red-flower set.
terms.connects: This is the starting point; it guarantees a non-empty intersection of roses and red flowers.

terms.term: Statement ii: All red flowers fade quickly
terms.meaning: Universal affirmative; every red flower is also in the fade-quickly set.
terms.example: The red-flower set is entirely contained in the fade-quickly set.
terms.connects: This is the key bridge; it moves properties from the red-flower set to the fade-quickly set.

terms.term: Statement iii: Some roses fade quickly
terms.meaning: Particular affirmative; at least one rose is also in the fade-quickly set.
terms.example: At least one object is simultaneously a rose and a fade-quickly item.
terms.connects: This is the candidate conclusion derived from combining i and ii.

given.plan: Check option C: if some roses are red (i true) AND all red flowers fade quickly (ii true), then those red roses must fade quickly, making iii true. Then show options A, B, D are not guaranteed by constructing counterexamples.

steps.title: Verify option C: (i) true AND (ii) true => (iii) true
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (B -> C), conclude (A -> C)
steps.apply: Let R = roses, Red = red flowers, F = fade quickly. Premise i: some roses are red, so (R intersect Red) is non-empty. Let x be a rose that is red. Premise ii: all red flowers fade quickly, so x (being red) also fades quickly. Therefore x is both a rose and fades quickly. Hence (R intersect F) is non-empty, which is exactly statement iii. So if both i and ii are true, iii must be true.
steps.note: This is the standard 'Barbara' syllogism pattern applied to a particular-universal combination.

steps.title: Disprove options A and B: (i) true, (ii) false does not force (iii) either way
steps.formula_raw: Statement R implies statement C when every interpretation (model) that makes R true also makes C true.
steps.apply: If ii is false, then some red flowers do NOT fade quickly, but that says nothing definitive about roses fading. The red roses might or might not fade for other reasons. We can construct a model where (i) is true and (iii) is true (roses fade for non-color reasons), and another where (i) is true and (iii) is false. So neither 'iii is false' (option A) nor 'iii is true' (option B) is guaranteed.
steps.note: Options A and B are both wrong because (iii) is indeterminate when (ii) is false.

steps.title: Disprove option D: (i) false AND (ii) false does not force (iii) false
steps.formula_raw: Statement R implies statement C when every interpretation (model) that makes R true also makes C true.
steps.apply: Even if no roses are red (i false) and not all red flowers fade (ii false), roses might still fade quickly due to non-color reasons. For example, all roses could fade quickly due to being delicate flowers regardless of color. So (iii) can be true even when both (i) and (ii) are false. Option D is INVALID.
steps.note: The falsity of the premises does not force the conclusion to be false; other causal paths remain.

solution.result: Only option C is a valid logical inference. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Hypothetical syllogism confirms option C by chaining the particular affirmative i with universal affirmative ii; fol-implication-validity is used to disprove options A, B, and D by counterexample.

questions.question:
Some students were not involved in the strike.
If the above statement is true, which of the following conclusions is/are logically necessary?
1. Some who were involved in the strike were students.
2. No student was involved in the strike.
3. At least one student was involved in the strike.
4. Some who were not involved in the strike were students.

A. 1 and 2
B. 2
C. 4
D. 2 and 3
questions.to_find: Which conclusion(s) are logically necessary given that some students were not involved in the strike

understand.plain: The given statement tells us exactly one thing: there exists at least one student who did NOT participate in the strike. This is a particular negative about a subgroup of students. We must carefully distinguish what is guaranteed versus what is merely possible. The statement says nothing about students who were involved — some students could have been involved, none could have been involved, or all other students could have been involved. Only conclusion 4 directly restates the given information.

keywords.term: Particular negative (Some A are not B)
keywords.explain: Asserts that at least one member of set A is NOT in set B, leaving open whether other members of A are or are not in B.
keywords.example: Some students were not in the strike — at least one student is outside the strike set.

keywords.explain: A conclusion that must be true in every possible scenario that makes the premise true; it cannot be false when the premise is true.
keywords.example: Conclusion 4 restates the premise directly, making it necessary.

keywords.explain: When a premise is consistent with multiple contradictory scenarios, no conclusion that distinguishes those scenarios is logically necessary.
keywords.example: The premise is consistent with both 'no other students were involved' and 'some other students were involved', so conclusions 1, 2, 3 are not necessary.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 380 170' width='380' height='170'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='380' height='170' rx='10' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='190' cy='80' rx='155' ry='60' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='190' y='25' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle' font-size='14'>All People</text><ellipse cx='150' cy='85' rx='80' ry='45' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='85' y='82' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12'>Students</text><ellipse cx='230' cy='85' rx='80' ry='45' fill='none' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='305' y='82' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12'>Strike</text><rect x='60' y='72' width='50' height='26' rx='5' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='85' y='90' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' font-size='11'>KNOWN</text><text x='190' y='155' class='tag mut' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12'>Shaded region (students not in strike) is non-empty — guaranteed</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram with two overlapping ellipses (Students and Strike). The non-overlapping part of Students is highlighted to show it is non-empty per the given statement.

given.aim: Determine which conclusions must be true whenever 'Some students were not involved in the strike' is true

terms.term: Given: Some students were not involved in the strike
terms.meaning: At least one student is NOT a member of the strike-involved group.
terms.example: In the Venn diagram, the Students-only region (outside the Strike circle) is non-empty.
terms.connects: This is the only guaranteed fact; everything else about the overlap or non-overlap is uncertain.

terms.term: Conclusion 4: Some who were not involved in the strike were students
terms.meaning: At least one person in the not-involved group is a student.
terms.example: The same student(s) from the given statement — they are not involved and they are students.
terms.connects: This is just the given statement rephrased: the student(s) not in the strike are members of the not-involved group who are students.

terms.meaning: Conclusions about students who WERE involved are not forced by the given premise.
terms.example: It is possible that zero other students joined the strike (making 1 and 3 false), or that all remaining students joined (making 2 false); both scenarios satisfy the given statement.
terms.connects: The given statement is consistent with both 'no students in strike' and 'some students in strike', so none of 1, 2, 3 is logically necessary.

given.plan: Restate the given conclusion in logical form; check each numbered conclusion to see if it holds in every model consistent with the premise; eliminate those that fail in at least one model.

steps.title: Formalize the given statement
steps.formula_raw: "All A are B" = forall x (A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: Given: exists x such that x is a student AND x is not involved in the strike. Let S = set of students, I = set of people involved in the strike. Given: (S minus I) is non-empty, i.e., there exists at least one person in S who is not in I.
steps.note: This is a particular negative: some students are outside the strike set.

steps.title: Evaluate conclusion 4
steps.formula_raw: Statement R implies statement C when every interpretation (model) that makes R true also makes C true.
steps.apply: Conclusion 4: 'Some who were not involved in the strike were students' = exists x such that x is not in I AND x is a student. This is logically identical to the given statement (just the conjunction reordered). So in every model where the given statement is true, conclusion 4 is also true. Conclusion 4 is NECESSARY.
steps.note: Conclusion 4 is a direct restatement of the premise — it must be true whenever the premise is true.

steps.title: Eliminate conclusions 1, 2, 3
steps.formula_raw: Statement R implies statement C when every interpretation (model) that makes R true also makes C true.
steps.apply: Conclusion 2 ('No student was involved') and Conclusion 3 ('At least one student was involved') are contradictory — they cannot both be necessary. The given statement allows models where zero students joined the strike (making 1 and 3 false, 2 true) AND models where some students joined (making 1 and 3 true, 2 false). Since each conclusion fails in at least one valid model, none of 1, 2, 3 is logically necessary.
steps.note: When two conclusions contradict each other, at most one can be necessary; here the premise is consistent with both, making neither necessary.

solution.result: Only conclusion 4 is logically necessary. Answer: C. 4.

questions.formula_note: The fol-implication-validity check confirms conclusion 4 is necessary (it is the premise restated) and eliminates 1, 2, 3 by showing the premise admits contradictory models about student involvement.
